**Mustard greens**, sometimes called **Chinese mustard**, **Indian mustard**, **gai choi**, or **leaf mustard**,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) are the edible leaves of the *Brassica juncea* plant,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) which is closely related to cabbage.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) The leaves of some other brassicas (e.g. wild mustard) may be edible and referred to as mustard greens,[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) but this chapter covers *Brassica juncea* in particular.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Looking something like other leafy brassicas, if more delicate,[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) mustard greens come in heads of dark green leaves, each with a central stalk.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) The leaves are fluted or ruffly,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) and like the mustard seeds to which they are related, they have a slight pungency of flavor and bitterness.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

A few varieties of mustard greens exist,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) including large-petiole mustard *(dai gai choy)*, head mustard, and snow mustard.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

* ![Korean red mustard](/wiki/File:Brassica_juncea.jpg "Korean red mustard")

  Korean red mustard
* ![Gai choi](/wiki/File:Gai_Choi_Mustard_Greens_-_J_K_Asian_Grocery_(5050526668).jpg "Gai choi")

  Gai choi

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

As with other fresh greens, mustard greens should be crisp and bright green, without wilting or discoloration.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) Smaller and younger plants will be slightly less bitter than larger and older ones.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) They will keep, wrapped in plastic, for several days in the fridge.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Leafier varieties have a slightly shorter shelf life.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Being bitter, these greens can be eaten raw but are often cooked,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) which reduces their bitterness.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) More tender leaves are suitable for stir-frying, and varieties with thicker stems are used for pickling in Chinese and Southeast Asian cuisines.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) The greens are also steamed, braised, and incorporated into soups.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

Blanching the greens before cooking separately can help reduce the bitterness.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)
